Title: Gain-of-function of the 1-aminocyclopropane-1-carboxylate synthase gene ACS1G induces female flower development in cucumber gynoecy

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes female flower development/gynoecy in cucumber
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts S-adenosyl-L-methionine to ACC, which is then oxidized to ethylene by ACO2
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Predominantly expressed in early floral buds at stages 1-2, especially beneath the central zone of the floral meristem/carpel region; weak at stage 4
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Strong promoter activity in stages 1-3 floral buds; dominant transcript in pre-stage 4 female-destined buds in F-locus plants
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Ethephon/ethylene context used to support ethylene-mediated pathway; greenhouse-grown plants
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Gain-of-function ACS1G is necessary and sufficient to induce gynoecy by promoting ethylene production in early floral buds
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ACS1G, not MYB1 or BCAT, is the F locus gene conferring gynoecy. 2. ACS1G gained a novel promoter through duplication/recombination and is expressed early in floral bud development. 3. ACS1G transcripts dominate over ACS1F/ACS1f at the key stage of female sex determination. 4. Transgenic introduction of the ACS1G genomic region converts monoecious cucumber to gynoecious. 5. ACS1G requires ACO2 to generate the ethylene burst that represses WIP1 and activates ACS2.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR/Cas9 mutagenesis; EMS/TILLING alleles distinguishing transcripts; recombinant enzyme assay; in situ hybridization; promoter-GUS assay; transgenic complementation with genomic ACS1G; genetic segregation with aco2
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value dominant gynoecy gene for hybrid seed production and yield improvement; deploy by introducing/editing the ACS1G promoter-expression module rather than increasing ACS1 copy number; ensure functional ACO2 background for effectiveness
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No explicit growth penalty reported; gynoecious lines produce few male flowers, complicating seed maintenance and mutagenesis
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native ACS1G promoter (novel promoter created by BCAT-derived sequence); transgenic genomic ACS1G region with ~4.5 kb upstream; ProACS1G:GUS native promoter reporter
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ACS1G shares identical coding sequence with ACS1F/ACS1f; its female-promoting activity is due to altered promoter-driven spatiotemporal expression rather than enzyme sequence or dosage alone.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: YB-Gy; monoecious and gynoecious isogenic lines; 406a mutant background
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Required for female flower initiation by enabling ethylene production
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes ACC to ethylene in the final step of ethylene biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Strongly expressed in central region of stage-2 floral buds in gynoecious lines; overlaps ACS1G expression; present along receptacle at later stage in floral buds
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Overlaps with ACS1G in early floral buds of gynoecious plants; lack of functional ACO2 causes androecy even with ACS1G or wip1 backgrounds
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ACC application does not rescue aco2 mutants; greenhouse-grown plants
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Functional ACO2 is required for ACS1G-dependent and WIP1-loss-dependent female flower development by enabling ethylene formation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ACO2 expression spatially overlaps with ACS1G in stage-2 gynoecious floral buds. 2. ACS1G plants with aco2/aco2 are androecious, showing ACS1G depends on ACO2. 3. wip1 aco2 double mutants fail to produce female flowers, indicating ACO2 is necessary for ACS2 activation and female development.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In situ hybridization; genetic segregation analysis in F2; double-mutant phenotyping; prior ACC rescue test referenced in study
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential enabling allele for deploying ACS1G-mediated gynoecy; should be preserved or co-selected in breeding/editing programs targeting sex determination
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of ACO2 causes androecy and blocks female flower formation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts as a basal ethylene-biosynthetic player; ACS1G and ACS11 require ACO2 to produce the ethylene burst needed for female development.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: CU2 backgrounds; gynoecious vs monoecious lines
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Provides ethylene necessary for carpel development and female flower fate
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts S-adenosyl-L-methionine to ACC during female flower developmental program
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: terminal determinant downstream in regulatory system
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Upregulated in gynoecious plants lacking functional WIP1; activated downstream of ACS1G/ACS11-ethylene signaling
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher in CU2 gynoecious wip1 background than monoecious WIP1 background
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Dependent on upstream ethylene and functional ACO2
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ACS2 activation is essential downstream output promoting sufficient ethylene for female flower development
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ACS2 expression is dramatically upregulated in gynoecious plants lacking functional WIP1. 2. The study model positions ACS2 as the last player in the FAM system and essential for female flower development. 3. ACS1G-generated ethylene activates ACS2, helping establish gynoecy.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR; genetic analysis/model integration from current and cited experiments
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential downstream engineering node to stabilize female flower formation, but regulation is complex and may require coordinated manipulation of WIP1/ACO2/ACS genes
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No explicit tradeoff reported; absence of ACS2 can lead to bisexual rather than fully female flowers according to model
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although central in the model, ACS2 is not the primary discovery target of this paper; included because direct expression changes were experimentally shown in Results.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: WIP family C2H2 zinc finger transcription factor
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: YB and 09 isogenic backgrounds; CU2 WIP1/wip1 lines
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Ethylene promotes female flower development partly by repressing WIP1
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Ethylene-mediated regulation of female flower developmental program via control of ACS2 expression
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: transcriptional repression of downstream female-promoting pathway; itself repressed by ethylene/ACS pathway
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Represses ACS2; reduction of WIP1 relieves ACS2 repression
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Strong repressor in the sex-determination network; its repression is a key step linking ethylene to ACS2 activation and female flower development, though not fully sufficient without ACO2
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Ethylene; ACS1G activity; ACS11 activity; ethephon treatment
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Part of feedforward ethylene pathway where ACS1G/ACS11+ACO2-derived ethylene represses WIP1, enabling ACS2 activation
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Significantly lower in gynoecious than monoecious isogenic lines; downregulated by ethephon in shoot apices
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Reduced in gynoecious lines and after ethephon treatment
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: WIP1 repression is associated with female flower development by permitting ACS2 upregulation, but WIP1 loss alone still requires ACO2 for full female development
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. WIP1 expression is significantly reduced in gynoecious lines compared with monoecious lines. 2. Exogenous ethephon significantly downregulates WIP1 expression. 3. Repression of WIP1 explains part of the ACS1G ethylene effect, relieving repression of ACS2. 4. However, WIP1-independent pathways likely also contribute because F_ wip1 plants still show strong female phenotype and wip1 aco2 mutants are not female.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR in isogenic lines; ethephon treatment; genetic interaction analyses with ACS1G, acs11, and aco2 backgrounds
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful complementary target for female-flower engineering, but editing WIP1 alone may be insufficient unless ACO2-dependent ethylene signaling remains functional
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: wip1 mutants can produce bisexual flowers at lower nodes; recessive behavior limits straightforward deployment compared with dominant ACS1G
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper treats WIP1 mainly as downstream/background regulator in the established FAM model, but provides direct supporting expression and genetic evidence in Results.
